preFix.title: Coil smoothing and wall blending
preFix.subtitle: Joining and smoothing coil joints into a continuous wall surface
preFix.excerpt: Coil pots are built by stacking clay ropes, but the visible seams between coils are a structural vulnerability as well as an aesthetic issue. This technique covers the interior and exterior blending methods that turn a stack of coils into a continuous, strong wall. Applies to air-dry clay and kiln-fired clay equally.
preFix.sourceNotes: Coil blending is a fundamental technique in all coil-building traditions worldwide. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: A
coil

pot is structurally only as strong as its joints. Unblended coil seams are crack lines waiting to open under the mechanical stress of drying or handling. Blending converts separate coils into a single homogeneous wall by physically merging the clay across the joint. The interior is the critical surface: a well-blended interior with a textured exterior is stronger than a smooth exterior with an unblended interior.
When to use it
Blend each coil immediately after placing it, before it stiffens to
leather-hard
. The window for blending is while the clay is still plastic and damp. If a coil has already stiffened, re-wet the joint with a damp sponge and apply fresh
score and slip
before pressing. Never attempt to blend bone-dry clay: the coils have no plasticity and blending pressure cracks them.
What you need
A wooden rib or metal rib for exterior blending
A fingertip or a damp sponge for interior blending
A small amount of slip (clay dissolved in water to a thick-cream consistency)
A needle tool for scoring if joining a stiffened coil
Step-by-step

Place a coil on top of the wall, pressing it down firmly to make full contact along the joint. The coil should be at the same moisture level as the wall below it: two damp surfaces blend; a dry coil on a damp wall forms a weak boundary.

Interior blending first: reach inside the pot with a fingertip and press clay from the upper coil downward onto the coil below, using a circular smearing motion. Work around the full circumference of the joint, blending about 10 mm up the new coil and 10 mm down into the coil below.
The interior blending is the structural join. It is more important than the exterior. A smooth interior wall, even if the exterior retains coil texture, is a fully bonded structure.

Exterior blending for a smooth finish: press the wooden rib against the exterior wall and drag it upward in short overlapping strokes. The rib moves clay over the joint line and smooths it away. Work around the full circumference. The exterior can be left with visible coil texture as a decorative choice; the structural strength comes from the interior blend.
After blending, check the wall thickness with fingers on both sides. A well-blended wall should be an even 6-8 mm throughout with no thin spots at the joints.
What it looks like when it is working

Running a fingertip along the interior wall of a well-blended coil pot should feel like a continuous surface with no ridges or seam lines. Pressing inward at a joint should not feel any weaker than pressing at the mid-coil. When the pot dries, no cracks should appear at the coil seams.
Common mistakes
Blending only the exterior: the interior joint remains unmerged and cracks at the coil seam during drying. Always blend the interior first.
Adding too many coils before blending: the lower coils stiffen and will not blend with the upper ones. Blend each coil before adding the next.
Using a dry fingertip on the interior: the finger drags rather than smooths. Wet the fingertip slightly with water before interior blending.
Pressing too hard on the exterior with the rib: this pushes clay inward and creates a thin wall at the joint. Short, light overlapping strokes are better than heavy dragging.
